---
pagetitle: KDE Analysis | Capstone
title: KDE Analysis using SpatialKDE package
---

![](../../Images/3.png){width="500"}

# Getting Started

The code chunk below uses `p_load()` function of pacman package to check if the required packages have been installed on the computer. If they are, the packages will be launched.

The packages used are: 

- sf package is used for importing, managing, and processing geospatial data. 
- tidyverse package for aspatial data wrangling. 
- SpatialKDE package to perform 1st-order spatial point patterns analysis and derive kernel density estimation (KDE) layer.
- tmap package is used to visualize geospatial data on maps.

```{r}
pacman::p_load(sf, tidyverse, SpatialKDE, spatstat, tmap)
```

In the code chunk below, the [cleaned data files](DataPrep.qmd) are loaded into R.

```{r}
# Load base map
mpsz = st_read(dsn = "../data/geospatial", 
               layer = "MPSZ-2019") %>%
  st_transform(crs = 3414) %>%
  select(PLN_AREA_N, geometry) %>%
  group_by(PLN_AREA_N) %>%
  summarise() %>%
  ungroup()

# Load rds file
pickup_sf <- st_as_sf(read_rds("../data/rds/pickup_sf.rds"))
dropoff_sf <- st_as_sf(read_rds("../data/rds/dropoff_sf.rds"))
```

# KDE for Pick-up Locations

Selecting the optimal bandwidth when performing spatial Kernel Density Estimation (KDE) is crucial because it significantly influences the accuracy of the density estimates. The bandwidth determines the scale of smoothing: if the bandwidth is too large, the data may be oversmoothed which would obscure important spatial features. Conversely, if the bandwidth is too small, it may result in a noisy estimate that overemphasizes random fluctuations. 

```{r}
# Extract coordinates
coords <- st_coordinates(pickup_sf)

# Calculate standard deviation (sigma) for longitude and latitude
sigma_lon <- sd(coords[,1])  # Longitude is the first column
sigma_lat <- sd(coords[,2])  # Latitude is the second column

# Calculate Silverman's bandwidth for each dimension
n = nrow(pickup_sf)
bw_lon = 1.06 * sigma_lon * (n ^ (-1/5))
bw_lat = 1.06 * sigma_lat * (n ^ (-1/5))

# Use the average or maximum of the two bandwidths
bw = mean(c(bw_lon, bw_lat))
```

```{r}
pickup_kde <- pickup_sf %>%
  kde(band_width = bw, kernel = "quartic", grid = mpsz)

tm_shape(pickup_kde) +
  tm_polygons(col = "kde_value", palette = "viridis", title = "KDE for Pick-up Points")
```

# KDE for Drop-off Locations

```{r}
# Extract coordinates
coords2 <- st_coordinates(dropoff_sf)

# Calculate standard deviation (sigma) for longitude and latitude
sigma_lon2 <- sd(coords2[,1])  # Longitude is the first column
sigma_lat2 <- sd(coords2[,2])  # Latitude is the second column

# Calculate Silverman's bandwidth for each dimension
n2 = nrow(dropoff_sf)
bw_lon2 = 1.06 * sigma_lon2 * (n2 ^ (-1/5))
bw_lat2 = 1.06 * sigma_lat2 * (n2 ^ (-1/5))

# Use the average or maximum of the two bandwidths
bw2 = mean(c(bw_lon2, bw_lat2))
```

```{r}
dropoff_kde <- dropoff_sf %>%
  kde(band_width = bw2, kernel = "quartic", grid = mpsz)

tm_shape(dropoff_kde) +
  tm_polygons(col = "kde_value", palette = "viridis", title = "KDE for Drop-off Points")
```
